transcription:
3) When sending a message, a process
attaches its vector clock to the message.

4) When receiving a message, you take the
maximum of the recieved VC and your own local VC
and increment your own position by 1 because reciev
is an event.

pointwise maximum: Max ([1,12,4], [7,0,2]
= [7,12,4]

CSE138 Lecture #4

[figure]

Happens before vector clock
$e_1 \to e_2 \Longleftrightarrow VC(e_1) < VC(e_2)$
Vector clock rules:

All elements are ≤, one element must be <: Happens before
All elements are ≥, one element must be >: Happens after
Otherwise: Concurrent

Lamport Clocks use up less bandwidth in a network
than VCs, which could be significant.
- Also have to know number of total participants
(Some implementations could work around these shortcomings)

Protocol: A set of rules that processes use
to communicate with each other, telling you
what messages you can send, in terms of what
messages you've recieved → or what you can
do locally
Alice Bob Alice Bob

Safety Properties
Sending a message is something you do.
FIFO message delivery violation
P₁ P₂
[figure]
Receiving a message is something that happens to you
Delivering a message is
something you choose (or not)
to do with a message
you've recieved.
FIFO message delivery: If a process sends message
m₂ after message m₁, then a process delivering
both delivers m₁ first. (2 messages, same sender and rec.)

How to implement?

Sequence numbers (TCP)
        - Messages have id and sequence #s.
        - Messages are delivered when they are one more than
          the last sequence number.

What if a message gets lost?
        It breaks
        This strategy only works if you have reliable
        delivery (TCP does)

                    (TCP also does this)
Acknowledgements
        - Can only send one once an acknowledgement
is received
        - Slower, more steps and also a risk of ack being
dropped

Causal Message Delivery:
        If the send of a message m₁ happens before
        the send of a message m₂, then any process
        delivering both delivers m₁ first. (m₁ & m₂ could
        have different senders.)
                - All FIFO violations are causal delivery
                violations, (not vice versa)
        vector clocks can ensure causal message delivery

Can only send one once an acknowledgement is received
Slower, more steps and also a risk of ack being dropped

vector clocks can ensure causal message delivery
